video_title: Socialists Sweep NYC, China Catches Up in Coding, AI Memory Crunch, Micron's Blowout Quarter
gist: All-In ep 278 with guests Travis Kalanick and Gavin Baker: DSA socialists sweep NYC Democratic primaries; China's open-source GLM 5.2 reaches near-frontier coding performance at ~85% lower cost while US frontier models (Anthropic's Fable, OpenAI 5.6) sit in regulatory purgatory; DRAM/HBM is the dominant AI bottleneck driving Micron's blowout and consumer-electronics inflation; and the panel maps data-center economics, orbital compute, and how to absorb a coming wave of SpaceX/Anthropic/OpenAI/Cerebras IPO supply.

themes.theme: DSA takeover of the Democratic Party

insights.claim: The DSA's electoral base is relatively wealthy, downwardly-mobile white liberals, not working-class, poor, black or Hispanic voters whom the traditional Democratic Party courted, per Gavin Baker.
insights.speaker: Gavin Baker
insights.so_what: Reframes the socialist surge as a niche demographic phenomenon rather than a broad working-class movement, relevant to handicapping political/regulatory risk to capital and corporations.
insights.quote: the voting base of the DSA are relatively wealthy white liberals who are downwardly mobile. They're losing votes with working-class people.

insights.claim: Zohran Mamdani's singular political talent, not DSA ideas or AI dissatisfaction, is the main driver of the socialist ascendancy, making the movement vulnerable if Democrats find a competing charismatic candidate.
insights.speaker: Gavin Baker
insights.so_what: If the movement is personality-driven, its policy threat to markets may be less durable than feared and hinges on one figure.
insights.quote: he is the reason that the DSA is ascendant. not their ideas or not dissatisfaction with AI, but it's him

insights.claim: Even Democrats who do not lose primaries will shift their voting and rhetoric in the DSA direction to avoid being primaried, so the movement's policy influence exceeds its actual seat count.
insights.speaker: David Sacks
insights.so_what: Policy risk (taxation, public ownership of corporations, regulation) bleeds across the whole party, widening the affected universe beyond DSA-held districts.
insights.quote: regardless of how many DSA candidates actually get elected, the rest of the party is now responding to this and they're going to bend.

insights.claim: Israel is now a decisive wedge issue in Democratic primaries, with 80% of Democrats and 57% of under-50 Republicans disapproving of Israel, exemplified by pro-Israel incumbent Dan Goldman's upset loss to Brad Lander.
insights.speaker: David Sacks
insights.so_what: A generational collapse in Israel approval signals durable shifts in US foreign-policy alignment that affect defense, aid flows and geopolitical risk pricing.
insights.quote: if you look at polling 80% of Democrats now say they disapprove of Israel.

insights.claim: Canada, the UK and Australia veered toward socialism before the US and now show political instability, and their bans on social media for under-16s could reverse youth radicalization, per Chamath Palihapitiya.
insights.speaker: Chamath Palihapitiya
insights.so_what: Frames under-16 social-media bans (already in Florida) as a potential political-regime variable; an expanding US ban would be a regulatory headwind for social platforms.
insights.quote: Each of those three countries now have banned social media when you're 16 and under. And I think it's sort of like making sure the kid doesn't get addicted to the drug too early.

insights.claim: Under-16 social media bans are really a vehicle to force adult de-anonymization and enable a censorship regime, not primarily child protection, per Travis Kalanick.
insights.speaker: Travis Kalanick
insights.so_what: Age-gating laws carry hidden identity-verification mandates that threaten anonymous platforms like X; a regulatory risk to social media business models.
insights.quote: the real point of banning under 16 is so that you can force adults to identify themselves and deanonymize themselves so you can set up a fullscale censorship regime

themes.theme: China closing the AI model gap

insights.claim: China's Z.AI released open-weight GLM 5.2 (744B parameters, 1M context, MIT license) scoring 51 on the Artificial Analysis Intelligence Index, beating GPT 5.5 on a frontier coding benchmark and trailing Claude Opus 4.8 by under 1 point at ~85% lower API cost.
insights.speaker: Jason Calacanis
insights.so_what: Near-frontier open-weight coding capability at a fraction of the cost compresses pricing power for closed US frontier labs and accelerates open-source adoption.
insights.quote: Beat GPT 5.5 on the Frontier SWE coding benchmark. That's a software one. Trails Claude Opus 4.8 by less than 1 percentage point.

insights.claim: GLM 5.2's quality was substantially achieved via distillation, harvesting reasoning traces from US frontier APIs through masked accounts at scale, which is a cheat-sheet shortcut to the frontier, per Gavin Baker.
insights.speaker: Gavin Baker
insights.so_what: If catch-up depends on distilling US labs, the gap could reopen when those labs ship new models, but it also pressures frontier labs' ability to monetize via API access.
insights.quote: for sure distillation has happened. There's been an immense amount of distillation.

insights.claim: The future of enterprise AI is composable 'council of LLMs' architectures with a router sending most queries (potentially ~85%) to a cheap fine-tuned open-weight model and only the hardest to frontier models acting as conductors, per Gavin Baker.
insights.speaker: Gavin Baker
insights.so_what: Composable routing shifts economic value from frontier-model margins toward inference infrastructure, favoring chip/infra providers over closed-model rent.
insights.quote: you're going to have a what Andre Karpathy called a council of LLMs.

insights.claim: Open-source models shift economic value from frontier-lab margins to AI infrastructure providers; frontier tokens capture ~90% of economic value while open-source tokens are 80%+ of tokens processed, a ratio that may persist.
insights.speaker: Gavin Baker
insights.so_what: Quantifies the value-versus-volume split, supporting a long infrastructure / cautious-on-closed-model-margin stance.
insights.quote: frontier tokens are capturing 90% of the economic value and open source tokens are probably 80% plus of tokens processed

insights.claim: Nvidia is the de facto American open-source champion and could release a GLM-5.2-or-better model any time but holds back due to channel conflict with its model-maker customers, per Gavin Baker and David Sacks.
insights.speaker: Gavin Baker
insights.so_what: Latent Nvidia model capability is a strategic option that could be unleashed if customers (e.g. OpenAI) build rival chips, reshaping competitive dynamics.
insights.quote: Nvidia is the American open source champion... they can release GLM 5.2 or better whenever they want.

insights.claim: If frontier labs like OpenAI build their own chips (e.g. its Broadcom-built 'Jalapeno'), Nvidia may retaliate by launching its own competing AI model or selling OpenAI's chips to others, per Jason Calacanis.
insights.speaker: Jason Calacanis
insights.so_what: Vertical-integration moves by model labs into silicon could trigger Nvidia to compete downstream in models, a key supplier-customer game to watch.
insights.quote: Don't be surprised if Nvidia starts an OpenAI competitor. You heard it here first on allin.

insights.claim: China is on a strong indigenization push, claiming GLM5 was trained entirely on Huawei Ascend 910b clusters and Deepseek V4 on Huawei chips, aiming to package 'AI in a box' (Huawei-optimized models plus chips) sold globally at a fraction of US cost.
insights.speaker: David Sacks
insights.so_what: A cheap integrated Chinese hardware+model export bundle threatens US share of the global AI market, strengthening the pro-export policy argument.
insights.quote: they're going to take these Huawei chips. I'm going to take these Huawei optimized models... They're going to sell it at a fraction of the cost globally,

insights.claim: China is roughly 9 months behind on models (plus/minus 3) and ~24 months behind on silicon yet only a few months behind in total, and can rapidly close capability gaps by concentrating resources once a breakthrough is known, per David Sacks.
insights.speaker: David Sacks
insights.so_what: Implies US lead time is short and shrinking, so self-imposed slowdowns are strategically costly; supports pro-deployment, pro-export positioning.
insights.quote: the Chinese have been, I'd say, 9 months behind our models, plus or minus 3 months depending on capability.

insights.claim: Anthropic's Fable model has been rolled back and OpenAI's 5.6 is stuck in new government approval hoops, leaving a Chinese open-weight model as good as the currently-available US frontier models, per David Sacks.
insights.speaker: David Sacks
insights.so_what: US regulatory purgatory on top-tier models temporarily neutralizes the US lead, a near-term competitive and commercial risk for the frontier labs.
insights.quote: Fable has been rolled back and GBT 5.6 is trying to navigate these new approval hoops.

insights.claim: Anthropic's Dario Amodei has engineered a regulatory moat by advocating an 'FAA for AI' approval process, and Sacks argues the government should not reward this regulatory capture by delaying competitors.
insights.speaker: David Sacks
insights.so_what: A government model-approval regime would entrench incumbents with the resources to navigate it; investors should watch whether such a regulator forms.
insights.quote: Daario posted a blog just a few weeks ago saying he wants an FAA for AI.

insights.claim: The censorship and political bias baked into Chinese open models (e.g. refusing Taiwan/Tiananmen queries) is not fatal because US companies like Perplexity can fork and restore the content, per David Sacks.
insights.speaker: David Sacks
insights.so_what: Removes a perceived barrier to Western adoption of Chinese open models, increasing the competitive threat to US closed models.
insights.quote: American companies have taken Chinese models and then essentially worked around and basically fixed the censorship inside their own forked version.

themes.theme: DRAM/HBM as the dominant AI bottleneck

insights.claim: Micron's revenue grew ~4x year-over-year from $9B to $42B, beat expectations by 16%, raised Q4 guidance to $50B vs $43B, and its stock is up 10x, with Gavin Baker's 2025 HBM-maker call up 14x.
insights.speaker: Jason Calacanis
insights.so_what: Validates HBM/DRAM as the highest-return AI-cycle trade so far and signals the memory upcycle is accelerating, not peaking.
insights.quote: revenue up 4x 4x year-over-year 9 billion to 42 billion beat expectations by 16% big jump in guidance for Q4 50 billion versus 43 billion their stock is up 10x

insights.claim: DRAM (especially HBM DRAM) is the single most important AI bottleneck because memory capacity and bandwidth are foundational to every model's performance, even more than power, NAND, HDDs or networking, per Gavin Baker, who notes Elon is focusing Terafab on memory.
insights.speaker: Gavin Baker
insights.so_what: Directs capital toward the memory chokepoint over other 'bottleneck bro' candidates; memory is the constraint that sets AI buildout pace.
insights.quote: The bottleneck that matters is DRAM. And DRAM and HBMD RAM, this is the most important bottleneck

insights.claim: Micron signed supply-chain agreements with price floors and ceilings covering ~50% of revenue across just four customers, with floor pricing above prior-cycle peak gross margins, potentially transforming DRAM industry economics.
insights.speaker: Gavin Baker
insights.so_what: Contracted floor pricing above past peaks structurally de-risks the historically cyclical DRAM business and supports a re-rating thesis.
insights.quote: the floor pricing in these new contracts is ahead of prior cycle peaks from a gross margin perspective

insights.claim: Only three companies (Micron, SK Hynix, Samsung) can make AI-grade HBM DRAM and Micron's entire 2026 supply is sold out, while consumer-grade DRAM from China's IPO-bound CXMT will flood that separate market.
insights.speaker: Gavin Baker
insights.so_what: An oligopoly with sold-out capacity supports pricing power for AI-grade memory, while CXMT pressures only the commodity consumer segment.
insights.quote: for the DRAM you need in these AI servers, there are three companies that can make it. It's really hard to do.

insights.claim: DRAM is projected to be 30-40% of all hyperscaler capex next year, with hundreds of billions flowing straight to memory, per Gavin Baker.
insights.speaker: Gavin Baker
insights.so_what: Quantifies memory's massive share of AI capex, reinforcing the memory-makers thesis and the inflationary pull on the broader chip supply.
insights.quote: DRAM is probably going to be 30 to 40% of all hyperscaler capex next year.

insights.claim: AI demand is hoovering up DRAM and forcing consumer-electronics price hikes, with Apple raising the MacBook Neo from $699 to $799 (~14-15%) and Mac Studio up 25%, plus Xbox price increases, creating demand destruction in price-sensitive consumer hardware.
insights.speaker: Jason Calacanis
insights.so_what: Memory scarcity is inflationary for consumer devices and squeezes margins/units for hardware makers while AI demand stays price-insensitive.
insights.quote: Apple, which had not passed on those costs to customers, is having to pass those increases on.

insights.claim: DRAM-makers' stocks are cross-sectionally cheap relative to the rest of AI even though their industry structure has improved like wafer-fab-equipment suppliers (e.g. Lam Research) that already trade at large premiums, per Gavin Baker.
insights.speaker: Gavin Baker
insights.so_what: A valuation-gap argument: DRAM names could re-rate toward equipment-supplier multiples as contracted pricing improves their business models.
insights.quote: these these stocks still trade are cross-sectionally cheap relative to the rest of AI.

insights.claim: Standing up a 1-gigawatt terrestrial data center costs ~$35B in Nvidia semiconductors plus ~$25B in power and cooling equipment, much of the latter being inflationary human labor, per Gavin Baker.
insights.speaker: Gavin Baker
insights.so_what: Concrete unit economics for gigawatt buildouts; the labor-heavy $25B power/cooling portion is the inflationary, hard-to-deflate component.
insights.quote: to stand up a 1 gigawatt data center, it's $35 billion in semiconductors, Nvidia semiconductors, and it's $25 billion of power and cooling equipment.

insights.claim: Orbital compute becomes economic once reusable Starship cuts launch to ~$5B per gigawatt, making space ~$40B vs ~$60-70B terrestrially within a few years, deployed as laser-linked racks rather than monolithic structures, per Gavin Baker.
insights.speaker: Gavin Baker
insights.so_what: If launch deflates while terrestrial power/cooling inflates, orbital compute flips favorable and structurally advantages SpaceX's vertically-integrated stack.
insights.quote: When Starship is reusable, it's going to cost $5 billion to put a gigawatt of compute into space.

insights.claim: Forecasted AI energy consumption is grossly underestimated given ~40% of data centers have been contested since 2021 and demand is effectively infinite against meager supply, which pulls forward the economics favoring space and makes remaining terrestrial power 'hope diamonds,' per Chamath.
insights.speaker: Chamath Palihapitiya
insights.so_what: Scarce, sizeable (100s of MW to GW) terrestrial power assets command extreme premiums; orbital advantage accrues to SpaceX over hyperscalers.
insights.quote: Whatever is left on the ground, though, will be incredibly incredibly valuable. It'll be a diamond. These are diamonds.

insights.claim: Tesla filed a June 18, 2026 trademark for 'Megapod,' modular self-contained AI data-center hardware (servers, power distribution, cooling) rumored to be deployed at Tesla supercharger sites that already have land and power.
insights.speaker: Jason Calacanis
insights.so_what: Signals Tesla/SpaceX entry into prefab modular data centers, enabling ~90-day build cycles and a new compute-supply channel using existing infrastructure.
insights.quote: Here's the trademark for Megapod that came out. This is a uh filing date of 618 20226.

insights.claim: Prefab modular data centers (Dell racks, Vertiv modules, Tesla Megapods, Crusoe shipping-container units) enable ~90-day build cycles by prefabbing GPUs in a warehouse and craning them onto a concrete pad, vs multi-year traditional builds, per Chamath.
insights.speaker: Chamath Palihapitiya
insights.so_what: Compresses data-center time-to-energize dramatically, a competitive edge and a demand driver for modular hardware suppliers.
insights.quote: you have like a 90day build cycle which is unheard of. So that that's where these mega pods are coming from.

insights.claim: Distributed/peer-to-peer training is fundamentally uneconomic because efficiency drops by an order of magnitude or more when GPUs are not physically adjacent, while distributed inference (latency-tolerant) is viable, per Travis Kalanick.
insights.speaker: Travis Kalanick
insights.so_what: Separates the investable opportunity (distributed inference networks like Bittensor/Targon) from the unviable one (distributed training), guiding compute-network bets.
insights.quote: the distributed training stuff. If these things are far away from each other... the efficiency drops dramatically.

insights.claim: Inference is disaggregating into prefill (memory-capacity-bound, 'remembering') and decode (memory-bandwidth-bound, 'speaking fast'), letting operators pair Groq/Cerebras decode chips with old Nvidia H100s/A100s to extend GPU useful life to 7-12 years, per Gavin Baker.
insights.speaker: Gavin Baker
insights.so_what: Longer GPU economic lives lower financing costs and make the AI buildout more financeable, while boosting demand for specialized decode chips.
insights.quote: we're going to be using GPUs for seven years, 10 years, 12 years, and that's great because it lowers the cost to finance them

insights.claim: The binding investment variable for AI compute providers is how many megawatts of power they can energize, since monetization rates (~$15B token revenue per $35B gigawatt) are known, per Gavin Baker.
insights.speaker: Gavin Baker
insights.so_what: Reframes the key diligence question for compute names from competitive position to power-bring-on cadence; megawatts, not benchmarks, drive revenue.
insights.quote: what matters here is not where they sit competitively... but just how quickly can they bring on power.

themes.theme: IPO supply absorption and pricing dynamics

insights.claim: Gavin Baker estimates Anthropic would trade at roughly $3 trillion as a public company, ending this year well over $100B (revenue) and reaching 85% inference gross margins at scale.
insights.speaker: Gavin Baker
insights.so_what: A $3T public valuation anchor for Anthropic with 85% inference margins frames the size and profitability of the coming AI-lab IPO wave.
insights.quote: I think Anthropic is worth $3 trillion today and it's very important.

insights.claim: Global capital markets can easily absorb the coming ~$6T of SpaceX/Anthropic/OpenAI/Cerebras offerings because only small floats (e.g. 5% of $3T = $150B) are actually sold and value is merely shifting from private to public markets, per Gavin Baker.
insights.speaker: Gavin Baker
insights.so_what: Counters fears that the IPO wave will overwhelm markets; the float, not headline valuation, is what must be absorbed.
insights.quote: it's not hard for global capital markets to absorb 50 billion of an offering.

insights.claim: SpaceX may not produce the feared lockup-expiry sell wall because insiders have had semiannual liquidity for ~10 years and nearly half of employees bought on the IPO, per Gavin Baker, contra a New York hedge fund's short thesis.
insights.speaker: Gavin Baker
insights.so_what: Argues against shorting SpaceX on the lockup; pre-existing liquidity reduces pent-up selling pressure post-IPO.
insights.quote: everybody who's a SpaceX investor or employee has had a chance to sell every 6 months for the last 10 years.

insights.claim: Chamath warns the SpaceX sell-wall risk may be real because its annual liquidity rounds 8x'd to ~$350B last year, so holders sitting on an 8x gain in one year could create heavy selling.
insights.speaker: Chamath Palihapitiya
insights.so_what: Offers the bear case against Baker: a sudden 8x mark-up changes seller behavior versus the prior steady 20-30% annual step-ups.
insights.quote: last year it was like 350 350 billion last year. So you got an 8x in one year. You could have a lot of people selling, right?

insights.claim: A class of portfolio managers automatically sells any IPO that breaks deal price, triggering price-insensitive selling and short pile-ons, which is what drove Cerebras down after its first public quarter, per Gavin Baker.
insights.speaker: Gavin Baker
insights.so_what: Explains mechanical post-IPO drawdowns; breaking deal price can cascade regardless of fundamentals, a tradeable pattern for new listings.
insights.quote: if a stock breaks deal price, they sell it no matter what. They consider it a promise that was broken.

insights.claim: Cerebras at sub-$40B market cap could reach a ~$9B cloud run-rate exiting 2027 if it adds 50MW/month, but the OpenAI deal's revenue impact won't show until around Labor Day 2026 due to ~7-month chip-to-token lead times, per Gavin Baker.
insights.speaker: Gavin Baker
insights.so_what: Sets a concrete valuation/timeline framework for Cerebras; near-term results lag the headline OpenAI contract, explaining the post-IPO weakness.
insights.quote: the first time you're going to see the impact of this open AI deal at the earliest is probably around Labor Day.

insights.claim: IPOs should be priced via Dutch auction to avoid breaking deal price, because mispricing (over or under) is hard to avoid when issuers anchor to a target number, per Chamath and Gavin Baker, who cite Uber's $17B auction-cleared round.
insights.speaker: Chamath Palihapitiya
insights.so_what: Advocates auctions over underwritten bookbuilds to reduce post-IPO breaks; relevant to evaluating the quality of upcoming AI listings.
insights.quote: Auctions are the way. And so it's not hard to price if you just do it the way it's supposed to be done.
